metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the autoheal-vs-drain race: specify how the HEALTHCHECK or autoheal is suppressed/disabled for a container that is already in graceful shutdown (e.g. suppress autoheal when a SIGTERM has been received, use a separate shorter staleness threshold for HEALTHCHECK than for lander_down, or have the drain write a 'draining' sentinel the HEALTHCHECK respects).
findings.finding: Autoheal (AC4) can interrupt the drain (drain AC): during a 120 s drain the bot stops writing heartbeats; the HEALTHCHECK staleness threshold (implied ~90 s by AC5) will fire before the drain completes, marking the container unhealthy and triggering autoheal to issue a docker restart while the graceful shutdown is still in progress. This creates a race that defeats the crash-safe recovery guarantee the plan is designed to provide.
findings.location: AC4 (liveness/restart) vs. drain-on-shutdown + crash-safe recovery AC
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'draining' sentinel file the bot writes immediately on SIGTERM; update the HEALTHCHECK to treat the presence of this file as healthy (bypass staleness check) so autoheal never fires during a legitimate graceful shutdown.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the autoheal-interrupts-drain race: specify a mechanism (e.g., suppress autoheal checks during graceful drain via a 'draining' sentinel file that autoheal's health-check script detects, or configure autoheal's `AUTOHEAL_STOP_TIMEOUT` to ≥ 120 s so its own `docker restart` respects the drain window) so that an orderly SIGTERM drain can always complete and write `recovery.json` before the container is killed.
findings.finding: During a SIGTERM drain (up to 120 s), the bot stops writing heartbeats; the HEALTHCHECK staleness threshold (~90 s, from AC5's lander_down bound) will fire mid-drain. The autoheal sidecar issues `docker restart`, which does NOT respect docker-compose's `stop_grace_period: 120s` (that setting only governs `docker compose stop/down`, not an external sidecar's `docker restart`). Autoheal can therefore kill the draining bot — via a second restart signal — before it writes `recovery.json`, defeating the entire drain-and-recover guarantee.
findings.location: AC4 (Liveness/restart) + Drain-on-shutdown AC + docker-compose.yml stop_grace_period
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'draining' flag file (e.g., `/state/draining`) written at SIGTERM entry and deleted at exit; modify the HEALTHCHECK script to return healthy (exit 0) if the draining flag is present, preventing the autoheal sidecar from issuing a competing restart during the drain window.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete proving command for each of AC5, AC4, and the emergency-stop AC (e.g., for AC4: `sleep 100 && docker inspect autolander --format '{{.State.Health.Status}}' | grep -x unhealthy`; for emergency-stop: `touch state/emergency_stop && sleep 20 && curl .../status | jq -e '.phase == "paused"'`).
findings.finding: AC5 (heartbeat freshness), AC4 (HEALTHCHECK → unhealthy), and the emergency-stop AC each lack a concrete proving command; the only verification described is the consolidated crash-recovery test at the end, which does not cover these three ACs.
findings.location: AC5 (Heartbeat), AC4 (Liveness/restart), Emergency-stop AC

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a test AC for the emergency-stop sentinel covering: (a) sentinel creation transitions bot to `paused` and stops new-work selection; (b) sentinel removal resumes the loop; (c) sentinel present on restart is respected after crash-recovery reconciliation; (d) sentinel created mid-submit does not corrupt the in-flight wipChain.
findings.finding: The emergency-stop sentinel flow (operator creates file → bot transitions to `paused`, operator removes file → bot resumes consuming Autosubmit changes) is a new operator-facing behavior with zero test coverage in the plan. The sole test AC (crash-recovery/idempotency) covers only kill-point crash scenarios; it never exercises the sentinel lifecycle, the paused→running transition, or what happens to an in-flight wipChain when the sentinel is dropped mid-submit.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — Emergency-stop sentinel (AC paragraph 3) and Crash-recovery/idempotency test AC
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the test AC: 'An automated test creates the sentinel file mid-idle and asserts the bot enters `paused` (no new Autosubmit changes processed); removes the sentinel and asserts the bot resumes within one poll cycle; creates the sentinel mid-submit and asserts the in-flight wipChain either completes or writes a valid recovery.json before halting; and verifies a restarted bot that finds both recovery.json and the sentinel file respects the paused state before reconciling.'

findings.finding: The crash-recovery/idempotency test is silent on whether it exercises LIVE Gerrit and S3 or uses mocks/stubs. The `phase=submitting` reconciliation decision ('check LIVE Gerrit: all members MERGED → write merged outcome; not merged → re-drive') is the highest-risk path; if the test uses a mock Gerrit that short-circuits the live status-check, the actual live-reconciliation code path is never exercised end-to-end, and the suite can pass while the live path is broken.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — Crash-recovery/idempotency test AC; recovery decision rule for `phase=submitting`
findings.suggested_fix: Amend the test AC to read: 'The `phase=submitting` kill-and-restart case must be covered by an integration test that queries a real (or real-equivalent seam) Gerrit instance, or a named follow-on ticket must track the live end-to-end validation of the submitting-phase reconciliation path.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend the `phase=submitting` decision rule to cover the partial-merge case (some members MERGED, some not): specify the concrete action (e.g., treat as an error, increment `autolander_errors`, remove `Autosubmit` from remaining open changes, and emit a stuck-state alarm for operator intervention) so no code path is undefined.
findings.finding: The `phase=submitting` recovery decision rule handles only two outcomes — 'all members MERGED' and 'not merged' — but omits the partial-merge case: some (but not all) chain members are MERGED while others are not. For a dependent chain submit, Gerrit may merge some changes before failing on a later one (e.g., a merge conflict or FFO violation revealed at submit time). Re-driving in this state risks attempting to re-submit already-MERGED changes, which Gerrit will reject, or selecting a broken chain for re-submission.
findings.location: Drain-on-shutdown + crash-safe recovery AC — 'phase=submitting (mid-submit)' decision rule

findings.finding: The `phase=submitting` recovery path contains a TOCTOU race that breaks the plan's double-submit safety guarantee: after a crash during `POST /submit`, Gerrit may still be processing the submit asynchronously. On restart the bot queries Gerrit, sees status `NEW` (submit enqueued but not yet complete), concludes 'not merged → re-drive,' and issues a second `POST /submit`. The plan's decision rule ('not merged → re-drive') does not account for the window between submit dispatch and Gerrit completing the merge, and specifies no poll/wait before making the re-drive decision.
findings.location: AC: Drain-on-shutdown + crash-safe recovery — `phase=submitting` bullet
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the `phase=submitting` recovery bullet: 'On restart: poll Gerrit status for all chain members with exponential backoff (e.g., 2 s, 4 s, 8 s, 16 s, max 60 s total) before deciding; only if status is still not MERGED after the poll window has elapsed, proceed with re-drive. If any member reaches MERGED during the poll window, treat as a completed submit and write the merged outcome.'

findings.finding: 'mid-handback' is described as a kill-point phase in the recovery decision table but has no corresponding value in the S2b-defined phase enum `{selecting, rebasing, awaiting_verified, submitting, idle, paused}`. The plan therefore cannot specify what `phase` value is written to recovery.json when the bot is killed during handback, making the recovery dispatch rule unexecutable.
findings.location: AC: Drain-on-shutdown + crash-safe recovery — 'mid-handback' bullet; S2b AC1 (`wipChain` schema)
findings.suggested_fix: In S2b AC1, extend the enum to `phase ∈ {selecting, rebasing, awaiting_verified, submitting, handback, idle, paused}`. In S5d, update the recovery bullet to read 'phase=handback — complete the idempotent Autosubmit-removal ...' so the dispatch condition maps 1-to-1 with a writeable enum value.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a test file entry (e.g. `tests/test_crash_recovery.py` — CREATE) to the Files section to cover the crash-recovery/idempotency test AC.
findings.finding: The crash-recovery/idempotency test AC describes an automated test deliverable (killing the bot at each phase and asserting convergence) but no test file is listed in the Files section — this deliverable is uncovered by the plan's file manifest.
findings.location: Files section vs. crash-recovery/idempotency test AC (last AC)
findings.suggested_fix: Add `tests/test_crash_recovery.py — CREATE: parametrized crash-injection test for each kill-point phase (rebasing, awaiting_verified, submitting, mid-handback); asserts no double-submit, no stranded stack, and S3 marker converges to acknowledged=true.` to the Files section.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that recovery.json is written atomically (write to a `.tmp` sibling then `os.replace()` / `rename(2)`) so a partial write or SIGKILL never leaves a corrupt file on the state volume.
findings.finding: recovery.json is written during the SIGTERM drain but the plan specifies no atomic write semantics (e.g., write-to-tmpfile then rename). If Docker SIGKILLs the process at 120 s while the write is in progress, or if the write fails partway (disk full, I/O error), the resulting partial/corrupt recovery.json will be read by the restarted bot and could produce undefined reconciliation behavior — potentially stranding the wipChain or triggering a double-submit.
findings.location: AC: Drain-on-shutdown + crash-safe recovery — 'immediately writes a concrete recovery record (recovery.json in the state volume)'
findings.suggested_fix: In loop.py's SIGTERM handler: write to `recovery.json.tmp` in the same directory, then call `os.replace('recovery.json.tmp', 'recovery.json')`. Also wrap the write in a try/except that logs a CRITICAL error and falls back to discarding state (re-select from scratch) if the write itself fails, rather than silently leaving a partial file.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a specified retry/backoff policy (e.g., exponential backoff up to N attempts, then enter `paused` phase with an operator alert) for Gerrit and S3 calls made during restart reconciliation, so the bot never silently skips reconciliation or hangs indefinitely.
findings.finding: The restart reconciliation step reads recovery.json and then 'reconciles against LIVE Gerrit and S3's markers BEFORE selecting new work', but the plan specifies no error handling or fallback for the case where Gerrit or S3 is unreachable at reconciliation time. If either dependency is down at restart, the bot could loop indefinitely, crash, or — worst — skip reconciliation and select new work without knowing the previous wipChain's fate.
findings.location: AC: Drain-on-shutdown + crash-safe recovery — 'On restart the bot reads recovery.json + reconciles against LIVE Gerrit and S3's markers BEFORE selecting new work'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Require that `recovery.json` is written atomically (write to a sibling `.recovery.json.tmp` then `os.replace()` / `rename()`) and specify the restart behaviour on a missing or unparseable recovery file (e.g., log a warning and treat as no prior state, which is safe for rebasing/awaiting_verified but requires a live-Gerrit check for submitting).
findings.finding: The plan does not require atomic write semantics for `recovery.json`. If the bot is SIGKILL'd mid-write (e.g., drain exceeds 120 s and Docker delivers SIGKILL, or the host OOMs), the file is left truncated or partially written. On restart the bot reads a corrupt `recovery.json` with no specified parse-failure handling, and could either crash (no recovery at all) or silently use partial data (wrong phase/chain, leading to double-submit or strand).
findings.location: Drain-on-shutdown + crash-safe recovery AC — 'immediately writes a concrete recovery record (recovery.json in the state volume)'
findings.suggested_fix: Write to `/state/.recovery.json.tmp`, flush, then `os.replace('/state/.recovery.json.tmp', '/state/recovery.json')`. On restart, if JSON parse fails: delete the corrupt file, log `autolander_errors++`, and treat as a fresh start (safe because the live-Gerrit reconciliation in the submitting check will catch any partially-submitted chain).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that `recovery.json` writes MUST be atomic (write to a sibling temp file, fsync, then rename into place) and that on restart the bot treats a missing or unparseable `recovery.json` as a safe 'no prior state' condition (re-select from scratch), documenting this fallback explicitly in the AC.
findings.finding: The plan does not specify that `recovery.json` must be written atomically (e.g., write to a temp file then rename). If the write is interrupted mid-stream — by an unexpected SIGKILL, disk-full, or crash within the 120 s grace window — the restart will find a corrupt or zero-length file. The plan provides no guidance on how the bot should handle a corrupt `recovery.json` on startup (ignore and re-select from scratch vs. crash-loop vs. treat as a specific phase). This breaks the resumability/idempotency guarantee that is the plan's central reliability claim.
findings.location: Drain-on-shutdown + crash-safe recovery (RELIABILITY FALLBACK) AC — `recovery.json` write and restart-reconciliation prose
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the AC: 'The write to `recovery.json` MUST be atomic: serialize to `recovery.json.tmp` in the same directory, call `fsync`, then `os.replace("recovery.json.tmp", "recovery.json")`. On restart, if `recovery.json` is absent or fails JSON parsing, the bot MUST log a warning and treat it as no-prior-state (re-select from scratch), which is safe because re-selection is idempotent for all phases except `submitting` — for `submitting`, absence of a readable recovery file forces a live Gerrit check before any submit attempt.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that consumers (HEALTHCHECK and S5c) treat an absent heartbeat file as healthy/unknown (not stale) for the first poll window (e.g., use Docker's `--start-period` on the HEALTHCHECK, and have S5c return `heartbeat_age_s: null` when the file does not exist).
findings.finding: The heartbeat file may be absent (never yet written) when the container first starts — before the first 15 s poll completes. The plan enumerates consumers (HEALTHCHECK stat, S5c endpoint) but does not specify how they should behave when the file is absent, only when it is stale. If the HEALTHCHECK treats 'file not found' as stale/unhealthy, the container will be marked unhealthy and restarted by autoheal before it can ever write the first heartbeat, creating a restart loop on every cold start.
findings.location: AC: Heartbeat (AC5) and AC: Liveness/restart (AC4)
findings.suggested_fix: Add `--start-period=30s` to the HEALTHCHECK instruction in Dockerfile.autolander so Docker does not evaluate liveness until after the first poll cycle; have the S5c handler return `{"heartbeat_age_s": null}` with a note when the file is absent.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend the emergency-stop AC to explicitly define the in-flight wipChain disposition: either 'complete the current phase step then pause' or 'write recovery.json and pause immediately' — and assert this behavior in the crash-recovery test.
findings.finding: The emergency-stop AC specifies that the sentinel file "STOPS consuming new Autosubmit changes" but does not define the behavior for a wipChain that is already in-flight when the sentinel is detected. It is unclear whether the bot (a) finishes the current chain before pausing, (b) immediately suspends mid-chain and writes recovery.json, or (c) abandons the chain. Without this specification, the implementation could strand an active stack.
findings.location: AC: Emergency-stop — 'transitions the bot to `paused` (the S2 `phase` value) — STOPS consuming new Autosubmit changes'
findings.suggested_fix: Recommended: 'On detecting the sentinel, the bot completes the current atomic phase step (e.g. finishes a rebase already in progress) but does NOT advance to the next phase — it writes recovery.json for the paused wipChain and sets phase=paused.' This is analogous to Zuul's graceful pause semantics.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the exact serialized string for the mid-handback `phase` value (e.g., `phase='handback_in_progress'`) and add it to the decision table alongside `rebasing`, `awaiting_verified`, and `submitting`.
findings.finding: The `phase` field in `recovery.json` has an incomplete value vocabulary: three phases are named as enum values in the decision table (`rebasing`, `awaiting_verified`, `submitting`) but the fourth kill-point is described only as prose 'mid-handback' — no concrete serialized enum label is given. An implementer cannot determine what string to write into `recovery.json` for this state.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — Drain-on-shutdown + crash-safe recovery, `recovery.json` schema + decision-rule table
findings.suggested_fix: Add a fourth row to the decision table: `phase=handback_in_progress` — complete the idempotent `Autosubmit`-removal across the stack keyed by `acknowledged=false`, then set `acknowledged=true`. Update the schema description to list all four valid phase values explicitly.

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.finding: The HEALTHCHECK staleness threshold is referenced by name ('unhealthy if older than the staleness threshold') but never defined numerically. AC5 defines two heartbeat thresholds (freshness ≤15 s; lander_down >90 s) but neither is explicitly designated as the HEALTHCHECK threshold, leaving the executor to guess.
findings.location: AC4 (Liveness/restart) — 'unhealthy if older than the staleness threshold'
findings.suggested_fix: In AC4, change to: 'unhealthy if the heartbeat file is older than 90 s (the same 6× poll threshold used by lander_down in AC5)'

findings.finding: The HEALTHCHECK staleness threshold (AC4: 'unhealthy if older than the staleness threshold') is never given a concrete value in the plan, while AC5 defines 90 s only for `lander_down`. If the implementer picks a different value for the HEALTHCHECK (e.g. 30 s or 60 s), the HEALTHCHECK could restart the bot before `lander_down` triggers (or vice versa), undermining the layered-reliability design.
findings.location: AC4 (Liveness/restart) and AC5 (Heartbeat)
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC4: 'the HEALTHCHECK uses the same 90 s threshold as lander_down (unhealthy if heartbeat file age > 90 s), ensuring both liveness layers trip at the same boundary.'

findings.finding: The HEALTHCHECK staleness threshold in AC4 is specified only as 'unhealthy if older than the staleness threshold' — no concrete value is given. AC5 gives 90 s for `lander_down`, but the HEALTHCHECK threshold must be a different (smaller) value to ensure autoheal restarts the container before `lander_down` fires downstream. Without a concrete value, the implementer must invent the threshold, and there is no guarantee the chosen value gives autoheal enough time to restart the container before the 90 s `lander_down` boundary.
findings.location: AC4: Liveness/restart — HEALTHCHECK bullet
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC4: 'HEALTHCHECK --interval=30s --timeout=5s --start-period=20s --retries=1 CMD stat the heartbeat file and check age < 45 s (3× poll).'

findings.finding: The plan does not specify the concrete staleness threshold used in the `HEALTHCHECK` stat command in `Dockerfile.autolander`, nor the HEALTHCHECK `--interval`, `--timeout`, `--start-period`, or `--retries` values. AC4 says 'unhealthy if older than the staleness threshold' but leaves the threshold implicit. The 90 s `lander_down` threshold (AC5) and the 15 s poll cadence suggest the HEALTHCHECK threshold should be somewhere between these, but the Dockerfile author has no authoritative value to implement against.
findings.location: AC4 (Liveness/restart) — Dockerfile.autolander HEALTHCHECK
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC4: 'HEALTHCHECK parameters: `--interval=30s --timeout=5s --start-period=60s --retries=3`; the stat check uses a staleness threshold of **60 s** (4× poll, between the 15 s write cadence and the 90 s lander_down threshold) so autoheal triggers before `land` declares the bot down but tolerates transient Gerrit-call delays.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State explicitly in the emergency-stop AC whether the bot finishes the in-flight wipChain before pausing or writes recovery.json immediately and halts mid-chain (the two behaviors have opposite safety trade-offs).
findings.finding: The emergency-stop AC specifies the bot stops consuming NEW Autosubmit changes but is silent on what happens to the in-flight wipChain at the moment the sentinel file is detected — the executor cannot decide whether to (a) finish the current chain and then pause, or (b) write recovery.json immediately and pause mid-chain.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — Emergency-stop AC
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence: 'If a wipChain is in-flight when the sentinel is detected, the bot writes recovery.json immediately and exits to paused state — it does NOT wait for the chain to complete, since a long CI wait would defeat the purpose of an emergency stop.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the retirement path for recovery.json: after successful reconciliation (all members verified and S3 marker set to acknowledged=true), the bot must delete or atomically rename recovery.json so subsequent normal restarts do not re-trigger recovery logic.
findings.finding: The plan has no retirement/cleanup path for recovery.json after successful reconciliation. Once the restarted bot reconciles and converges to a safe state, recovery.json remains on the state volume. A subsequent planned restart (e.g., a normal deploy with no crash) will re-trigger the recovery logic for a wipChain that has already been resolved, potentially causing spurious re-drives or incorrect phase transitions.
findings.location: AC: Drain-on-shutdown + crash-safe recovery — recovery.json lifecycle
findings.suggested_fix: After the reconciliation block converges (sets acknowledged=true), call `os.unlink('recovery.json')` (or move to a `recovery.json.done` archive with a timestamp) before entering the normal work-selection loop.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Declare explicit 'Depends-on' edges to S5c (for heartbeat_age_s) and to whichever ticket implements S3's marker infrastructure, so the executor knows these must be merged before the test AC can pass.
findings.finding: The crash-recovery/idempotency test AC references 'S3's marker' and implicitly depends on S5c's status endpoint serving `heartbeat_age_s`, but no explicit dependency edges on those sibling tickets (S5c, S3 marker infrastructure) are declared — this is VERIFY-COMMAND DEFECT #9.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — Crash-recovery/idempotency test AC; AC5 (heartbeat_age_s served by S5c)
findings.suggested_fix: Add a '## Dependencies' section listing: 'S5c (status endpoint, heartbeat_age_s field)' and the S3-marker ticket name.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or extend AC4) that exercises the full autoheal cycle end-to-end in the test environment: stall the heartbeat artificially, wait for autoheal to restart the container, and assert the restarted container is healthy and recovery.json was consumed.
findings.finding: The end-to-end path of 'container goes unhealthy → willfarrell/autoheal detects it → container is restarted → recovery.json is read → bot resumes correctly' is never exercised as a whole by any AC; the HEALTHCHECK and autoheal restart are individually mentioned but no AC proves the full integrated cycle in a live environment.
findings.location: AC4 (Liveness/restart), Crash-recovery/idempotency test AC

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a measurement note or spike result bounding the worst-case drain path (S3 marker write + recovery.json write) to confirm 120 s is sufficient, or make the timeout configurable with a stated default derivation.
findings.finding: The 120 s drain bound (stop_grace_period) is asserted with no measurement or derivation rationale — it is unknown whether 120 s is sufficient (or excessive) for the worst-case wipChain write path.
findings.location: AC: Drain-on-shutdown + crash-safe recovery — 'The drain is time-bounded to 120 s'
findings.suggested_fix: In the AC, add: 'Basis: S3 write P99 latency measured at < 5 s in staging; wipChain snapshot write is < 1 s; 120 s provides >10× margin. Timeout is overridable via DRAIN_TIMEOUT_S env var (default 120).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Make the heartbeat staleness threshold (90 s) and drain timeout (120 s) configurable via environment variables (e.g. HEARTBEAT_STALE_S, DRAIN_TIMEOUT_S) with the current values as documented defaults.
findings.finding: The 90 s heartbeat-staleness threshold and the 120 s stop_grace_period are hardcoded constants with no configurability mechanism — both are operational thresholds that will need tuning as poll cadence or infrastructure changes.
findings.location: AC5 (Heartbeat): 'lander_down treats age > 90 s as down'; AC: Drain — 'stop_grace_period: 120s'
findings.suggested_fix: In loop.py read `HEARTBEAT_STALE_S = int(os.getenv('HEARTBEAT_STALE_S', 90))` and `DRAIN_TIMEOUT_S = int(os.getenv('DRAIN_TIMEOUT_S', 120))`; reference these env vars in docker-compose.yml and the Dockerfile HEALTHCHECK interval.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a CLAUDE.md or ADR entry capturing: (a) the sentinel-file emergency-stop pattern and its 'survives redeploy' invariant, (b) the recovery.json schema with field semantics, and (c) the per-phase restart-reconciliation decision rules.
findings.finding: No ADR or design-doc update is planned for the novel sentinel-file emergency-stop pattern, the recovery.json schema/contract, or the multi-phase crash-recovery decision tree — all of which are non-obvious architectural choices that future maintainers must understand to modify safely.
findings.location: Files section; AC: Emergency-stop sentinel file; AC: Drain-on-shutdown + crash-safe recovery

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit observability requirement: when recovery.json is found on startup, the bot must log a structured WARN/ERROR event (including the recovered phase, change_id, and re_drive_count) and increment a named metric (e.g., `autolander_crash_recovery_total`) so operators and alerting systems can detect recovery events.
findings.finding: The plan specifies no observability instrumentation for the recovery path itself — there is no requirement to log, emit a metric, or alert when recovery.json is found and reconciliation is triggered on restart. An operator has no way to know that a crash-recovery event occurred, whether reconciliation succeeded, or which phase was recovered from, without reading container logs manually.
findings.location: AC: Drain-on-shutdown + crash-safe recovery — restart reconciliation section

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or extend the existing recovery AC) specifying that `recovery.json` is deleted/invalidated after successful reconciliation, and that the crash-recovery test asserts the file is absent after a clean convergence to `acknowledged=true`.
findings.finding: The plan never specifies that `recovery.json` is deleted (or archived/invalidated) after a successful restart-recovery reconciliation. A stale `recovery.json` left in the state volume after a clean run would trigger spurious recovery logic on the next restart, potentially causing the bot to re-drive an already-merged chain.
findings.location: AC: Drain-on-shutdown + crash-safe recovery — 'On restart the bot reads recovery.json + reconciles'
findings.suggested_fix: After completing reconciliation and writing the final S3 marker with acknowledged=true, delete or rename recovery.json (e.g. recovery.json.done) before exiting the recovery path. Add a test assertion that recovery.json is absent after a successful reconciliation run.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct `infra/compose/Dockerfile.autolander` from EDIT to CREATE and enumerate the required Dockerfile contents (base image, package install, entrypoint, HEALTHCHECK) so the full scope is visible.
findings.finding: The plan designates `infra/compose/Dockerfile.autolander` as an EDIT target, asserting the file already exists, but a directory probe of `infra/compose/` shows only `Dockerfile.gerrit`, `Dockerfile.reviewbot`, `docker-compose.yml`, and `gerrit.config` — no `Dockerfile.autolander` exists.
findings.location: Files section: `infra/compose/Dockerfile.autolander — EDIT`
findings.suggested_fix: Change to `infra/compose/Dockerfile.autolander — CREATE` and add a note specifying the base image (e.g., `python:3.12-slim`) and that this mirrors the structure of `Dockerfile.reviewbot` plus a `HEALTHCHECK` directive stat-ing the heartbeat file.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Change `infra/compose/Dockerfile.autolander` from EDIT to NEW (or add an explicit `depends_on: S5a` to S5d so the ordering constraint is declared), since the Dockerfile is created by S5a and does not yet exist.
findings.finding: infra/compose/Dockerfile.autolander is labeled EDIT in the Files section, but it does not exist in the repo and is created as NEW by sibling task S5a (08e1-5505-d898-4192). S5a is not declared as a depends_on of S5d. If S5d is worked before S5a lands, there is no file to EDIT for the HEALTHCHECK addition.
findings.location: Files section: `infra/compose/Dockerfile.autolander — EDIT: the HEALTHCHECK`
findings.suggested_fix: Either (a) add `depends_on: [08e1-5505-d898-4192]` to S5d's ticket so the ordering is enforced, or (b) consolidate the HEALTHCHECK into S5a's Dockerfile creation, removing the Dockerfile from S5d's edit set and keeping only loop.py and docker-compose.yml.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit `depends_on: S5a (08e1-5505-d898-4192)` to S5d so that the autolander service block (created by S5a) is guaranteed to exist before S5d edits docker-compose.yml to add `stop_grace_period: 120s`.
findings.finding: S5d's edit to docker-compose.yml (adding `stop_grace_period: 120s` to the autolander service) targets a service block that does not exist in docker-compose.yml until S5a lands its docker-compose.yml edit (which adds the autolander service). S5a is not declared as a depends_on of S5d, leaving this edit without a valid merge target if worked out of order.
findings.location: Files section: `infra/compose/docker-compose.yml — EDIT: set stop_grace_period: 120s on the autolander service`
findings.suggested_fix: Declare `depends_on: [08e1-5505-d898-4192]` on S5d's ticket, or note in the Files section: 'EDIT (requires S5a to land first — adds the autolander service block this edit targets)'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Pin a specific `willfarrell/autoheal` image tag (or confirm the ghcr.io variant) and verify that the `autoheal=true` container label and `AUTOHEAL_CONTAINER_LABEL` env-var API match that tag's documented interface before committing to it in AC4.
findings.finding: The plan asserts that `willfarrell/autoheal` restarts containers labeled `autoheal=true` on `unhealthy` status, but does not pin a version or verify that this label name and behavior is current for the image version being used. The `willfarrell/autoheal` image has had maintenance gaps and forks (e.g., `ghcr.io/willfarrell/autoheal`); the triggering label and Docker API assumptions differ between versions and may not be confirmed without a specific tag.
findings.location: AC4 — 'The `willfarrell/autoheal` sidecar (S5a) restarts the `autoheal=true`-labeled container on `unhealthy`'
findings.suggested_fix: In AC4 (and S5a's compose snippet), specify the exact image reference, e.g., `ghcr.io/willfarrell/autoheal:1.2.0`, and add a one-line note confirming the label name (`autoheal=true`) is the documented trigger for that tag.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Pin a specific `willfarrell/autoheal` image tag (e.g., `ghcr.io/willfarrell/autoheal:1.2.0`) in the plan and verify, against that tag's documented interface, that `autoheal=true` is the correct container label and that `AUTOHEAL_CONTAINER_LABEL` is not required as an env-var override.
findings.finding: The plan names `willfarrell/autoheal` as the restart sidecar and asserts a specific label contract (`autoheal=true` triggers restart on `unhealthy`), but specifies no image tag or registry. The project migrated from Docker Hub to `ghcr.io/willfarrell/autoheal`; the label key (`autoheal=true` vs an env-var override `AUTOHEAL_CONTAINER_LABEL`) and Docker API assumptions differ between versions and have not been verified against a pinned tag in this plan.
findings.location: AC4 — 'The `willfarrell/autoheal` sidecar (S5a) restarts the `autoheal=true`-labeled container on `unhealthy`'
findings.suggested_fix: In AC4 (and S5a's compose snippet), specify the exact image reference, e.g., `ghcr.io/willfarrell/autoheal:1.2.0`, confirm the label key from that version's README, and add a one-line verification note to the plan.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the maximum re_drive_count and the exceeded-policy (e.g., 'after 3 re-drives, abandon the wipChain, remove Autosubmit label with a comment, and resume normal selection') — or explicitly declare 'unbounded, operator must monitor'.
findings.finding: The `re_drive_count` field is included in the recovery.json schema and referenced in the decision rules but the plan never states a maximum re-drive limit or the behavior when that limit is exceeded — an executor must invent this policy.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — Drain-on-shutdown + crash-safe recovery AC, recovery.json schema

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define a maximum re-drive count (e.g., `re_drive_count >= 3`) after which the bot abandons the wipChain, moves it to a named error state, alerts operators, and selects new work.
findings.finding: The plan includes `re_drive_count` in the recovery.json schema but specifies no upper bound or circuit-breaker on re-drives. A persistently broken change (e.g., a change that always fails FFO-landability checks after a crash in `phase=submitting`) could cause the bot to re-drive indefinitely on every restart cycle, never making progress and blocking the entire queue.
findings.location: AC: Drain-on-shutdown + crash-safe recovery — 'phase=submitting: not merged → re-drive'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct `infra/autolander/loop.py` from EDIT to CREATE (or identify and declare the prerequisite story that creates the file/directory), and verify `infra/autolander/` is either created here or listed as a dependency.
findings.finding: The plan designates `infra/autolander/loop.py` as an EDIT target, asserting the file (and its parent directory `infra/autolander/`) already exist, but a directory probe of `infra/` shows no `autolander/` subdirectory at all — the file does not exist in the repo.
findings.location: Files section: `infra/autolander/loop.py — EDIT`
findings.suggested_fix: Change the Files entry to `infra/autolander/loop.py — CREATE` and add `infra/autolander/__init__.py — CREATE` to the Files list; or add an explicit dependency on the story that creates the autolander package scaffold.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify that S5c's status endpoint (which must expose `heartbeat_age_s`) is either already implemented and findable in the codebase, or explicitly listed as a prerequisite dependency of this story — do not leave AC5 dependent on an unverifiable assumption.
findings.finding: AC5 asserts that `heartbeat_age_s` is 'served … by S5c's status endpoint,' treating S5c's status endpoint as already-existing infrastructure this story depends on, but no S5c story, status endpoint, or `heartbeat_age_s` symbol is findable anywhere in the codebase.
findings.location: AC5 (Heartbeat): 'its age is served as `heartbeat_age_s` by S5c's status endpoint'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm that the `willfarrell/autoheal` sidecar service and `autoheal=true` label are added to `docker-compose.yml` either in this story or in S5a (which must be declared a prerequisite), and probe the S5a ticket to verify it covers this compose-stack change.
findings.finding: AC4 treats the `willfarrell/autoheal` sidecar as already deployed ('The `willfarrell/autoheal` sidecar (S5a) restarts the `autoheal=true`-labeled container'), but neither the autoheal service, the `autoheal=true` label, nor any reference to `willfarrell/autoheal` appears in `docker-compose.yml` or anywhere else in the repository.
findings.location: AC4 (Liveness/restart): 'The `willfarrell/autoheal` sidecar (S5a) restarts the `autoheal=true`-labeled container on `unhealthy`'
findings.suggested_fix: Add the autoheal sidecar service definition and the `autoheal=true` label on the autolander service to `docker-compose.yml` within this story's Files section (under `infra/compose/docker-compose.yml — EDIT`), since the plan already lists that file as an edit target.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief design note to the drain/recovery AC (or a linked ADR) explicitly justifying the bespoke `recovery.json` journal over established at-least-once queue patterns (e.g., SQS visibility timeout) given the single-instance, no-external-state-store constraint.
findings.finding: The bespoke `recovery.json` crash-journal + live-reconciliation pattern is a novel architecture for crash-safe at-most-once submission, but the plan never compares it against established at-least-once delivery primitives (e.g., SQS visibility timeout, transactional outbox, or a WAL) that already solve the 'never double-submit on crash-restart' problem with less bespoke reconciliation logic. Without this comparison, the reader cannot tell whether the custom design was chosen deliberately over these options or simply without considering them.
findings.location: AC — 'Drain-on-shutdown + crash-safe recovery (RELIABILITY FALLBACK)' and 'Crash-recovery/idempotency test'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a one-paragraph rationale, e.g.: 'SQS visibility-timeout would require an external queue and changes to S2's submit path; a WAL adds write-ordering complexity. For a single-instance bot where Gerrit is the durable source of truth, a lightweight local recovery record + live Gerrit reconciliation is simpler and sufficient. Revisit with external state store in the HA epic.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an IAM specification (a new `iam_s5d.tf` anchor or an explicit note that an existing policy covers it) granting the EC2 instance role scoped `s3:GetObject` and `s3:PutObject` on the specific S3 bucket (and key prefix) that holds the autolander outcome markers, and name that bucket in the plan.
findings.finding: The plan repeatedly invokes reading and writing 'S3's marker' (the outcome marker and `acknowledged` flag) during crash-recovery reconciliation, but never specifies which S3 bucket holds these markers, nor names or creates an IAM grant that allows the autolander process (running under the EC2 instance role `rebar-gerrit-instance-role`) to perform the necessary s3:GetObject / s3:PutObject operations. The existing IAM coverage in iam.tf covers only SSM reads and basic CloudWatch — no S3 data-bucket access. No iam_s5d.tf or equivalent anchor is listed in the plan's Files section.
findings.location: AC: 'Drain-on-shutdown + crash-safe recovery' — 'check LIVE Gerrit ... write the merged outcome/ack ... keyed by S3's acknowledged=false ... set acknowledged=true'; Files section lists no IAM file; infra/terraform/ has no iam_s5d.tf
findings.suggested_fix: Add an `iam_s5d.tf` file to the plan's Files list with an `aws_iam_role_policy` on `data.aws_iam_role.instance` (already declared in iam_s2.tf) that grants `s3:GetObject` and `s3:PutObject` scoped to `arn:aws:s3:::<marker-bucket>/autolander/*`. Also name the marker bucket (or reference the story that provisions it) so the resource ARN can be constructed without guessing.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Change `infra/autolander/loop.py` from 'EDIT' to 'NEW' (or 'NEW/EDIT depending on S2 delivery') and specify what the file must contain at creation, or explicitly note that S2 (perovskite-frilly-grebe) must land first and creates this file as a pre-condition of this plan.
findings.finding: The plan marks `infra/autolander/loop.py` as 'EDIT', implying the file and its parent directory already exist. However, a directory listing of `infra/` shows no `autolander/` subdirectory, and a search for 'autolander' across all infra files returns no matches. Applying an EDIT instruction to a non-existent file is a feasibility failure; this file and directory must be created (NEW), either by this plan or by its declared dependency (S2, perovskite-frilly-grebe).
findings.location: Files section — 'infra/autolander/loop.py — EDIT: heartbeat write, emergency-stop sentinel check, SIGTERM drain + recovery-record write, restart-recovery reconciliation.'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Depends on S2 delivering infra/autolander/loop.py' note in the Files section, or change the designation to 'NEW' with a stub spec of the module's structure (class, signal-handler hooks, and poll-loop entry point).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify when `re_drive_count` is incremented (e.g., at the start of each re-drive attempt), define a maximum (e.g., 3), and state the behavior when the cap is exceeded (e.g., abort the wipChain, write an operator alert, clear `recovery.json`, and re-select from scratch).
findings.finding: `re_drive_count` is listed as a `recovery.json` schema field alongside six other fields, but the plan specifies no increment rule (when it is written/incremented), no cap or ceiling value, and no behavior when the cap is reached. The field is present in the schema but has no associated processing protocol.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — Drain-on-shutdown + crash-safe recovery, `recovery.json` schema + `phase=submitting` decision rule

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a termination condition to the `phase=submitting` re-drive path: specify the maximum re-drive attempts (keyed by `re_drive_count`), and the fallback action when exceeded (e.g., abandon the wipChain, post a Gerrit comment, and re-select from scratch).
findings.finding: The `phase=submitting / not-merged → re-drive` fallback path ('re-verify FFO-landability, re-await, re-submit') specifies no exit/termination condition for the re-drive itself. If re-drive repeatedly fails (e.g., change is no longer FFO-landable, CI keeps failing), the plan provides no fallback sub-step, leaving the bot permanently stuck on a single wipChain.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — Drain-on-shutdown + crash-safe recovery, `phase=submitting` decision rule

findings.finding: The drain deadline is described as '~ the CI-wait timeout' but the plan never names a concrete duration or specifies that Docker's `stop_grace_period` must be set to accommodate it. Docker's default `stop_grace_period` is 10 seconds — far shorter than a CI-wait timeout (typically minutes to tens of minutes). Without specifying `stop_grace_period` in the compose service definition, Docker will SIGKILL the process after 10 s regardless of the bot's internal deadline, making the drain-and-write path unreachable in practice for any non-trivial CI-wait duration.
findings.location: AC: Drain-on-shutdown + crash-safe recovery — 'drain is time-bounded (SIGTERM deadline ~ the CI-wait timeout)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the docker-compose.yml autolander service spec: `stop_grace_period: 36m` (or whatever the CI-wait timeout + margin is). Add to the drain AC: 'SIGTERM deadline = CI-wait timeout + 60 s; stop_grace_period = SIGTERM deadline + 60 s.'

findings.finding: The SIGTERM drain deadline is described as '~ the CI-wait timeout' — asserted and vague, with no measurement plan and no Docker/compose stop_grace_period configuration specified. If the orchestrator's SIGKILL arrives before the drain deadline, recovery.json is never written and the crash-safe guarantee silently breaks. The crash-recovery idempotency test does not appear to exercise the deadline-exceeded path specifically.
findings.location: AC: 'Drain-on-shutdown + crash-safe recovery' — 'the drain is time-bounded (SIGTERM deadline ~ the CI-wait timeout)'
findings.suggested_fix: In `infra/compose/Dockerfile.autolander` or the compose file, set `stop_grace_period` to `<CI_WAIT_TIMEOUT + buffer>`. In the plan, state the concrete deadline (e.g. 'drain deadline = CI_WAIT_TIMEOUT − 30 s to leave time for recovery.json write'). Add a test case: inject a fake slow-CI hang, send SIGTERM, assert recovery.json is written within the deadline even when CI has not finished.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
